analysis:
- Algorithm families: the set splits into (A) exploratory population search (Heuristic 1), (B) greedy insertion/removal then local search (Heuristic 2), and (C) Pareto-filter → proxy ranking → bounded exact-eval greedy (CELF‑style) with small swap refinement (Heuristics 3–10). Family C dominates the quality/time tradeoff in these examples.
- Why family C is stronger: it prunes candidates via nondominated filtering and cheap box‑volume proxies, restricts exact hypervolume (HV) calls to a small shortlist, and uses lazy/CELF timestamps or eval caps to avoid redundant HV computations; it finishes with bounded swaps to capture easy improvements.
- Costly patterns to avoid: (1) blind evolutionary loops with very large T and frequent full‑set HV computations (Heuristic 1); (2) per-insertion brute-force removal HV for every member (Heuristic 2) which makes each step O(k)*HV; (3) rebuilding arrays, repeated try/except, and re-instantiating pg.hypervolume inside tight loops (common across many heuristics) — these add Python overhead and many allocations.
- Good data‑reuse and pruning practices seen: precomputing box‑volume proxies, caching marginal gains with (candidate, timestamp) tuples (CELF), capping shortlist sizes (adaptive to k), and limiting total HV evaluations. Heuristics that combine these (3–5) get best tradeoffs.
- Further micro‑optimizations missed or only partly used: more aggressive reuse of hv results (avoid re-creating pg.hypervolume objects each call), avoid stack reallocations by working with index lists/views, vectorized nondomination or sorting-based skyline algorithms, and deterministic seeding for reproducibility. Also, prefer upper bounds (box/proxy) to prune candidates before any exact HV evaluation.
experience: Favor Pareto filtering + cheap box‑volume/diversity proxies + CELF lazy greedy with timestamped cached marginals, an adaptive shortlist cap (O(k) or a small constant), and a tiny bounded swap refinement. Minimize HV calls by reusing data structures, avoid per‑candidate full HV scans, vectorize dominance checks, and bound total HV evaluations.
current_reflection: Favor Pareto filtering + cheap box‑volume/diversity proxies + CELF lazy greedy with timestamped cached marginals, an adaptive shortlist cap (O(k) or a small constant), and a tiny bounded swap refinement. Minimize HV calls by reusing data structures, avoid per‑candidate full HV scans, vectorize dominance checks, and bound total HV evaluations.
good_reflections: Prefer a hybrid constructive: filter dominated points, approximate contributions with vectorized Monte‑Carlo or fast tensor proxies to shortlist candidates, then apply exact HV only on the shortlist with cached/incremental updates and a small swap-based local search. Vectorize dominance precomputation, avoid rebuilding objects in loops, and keep all heavy work batched.

Next generation heuristics should: (1) pareto‑filter then shortlist by cheap proxy; (2) compute exact marginal HVC only on that shortlist using cached/incremental HV data structures; (3) vectorize or Monte‑Carlo only when needed; (4) cap HV evaluations and local search adaptively; and (5) reuse pygmo objects and add deterministic seeding and parallel shortlist evaluation.
bad_reflections: Favor a CELF lazy greedy with Pareto prefiltering and a box‑volume upper bound, limit candidates to O(max(k,200)), cache marginal gains with timestamps, and cap exact HV evaluations. Use small, budgeted swap refinements and avoid repeated array copies or full HV recomputations—this yields high HV for minimal extra runtime.

Design for minimal exact-HV calls: pareto-filter → precompute box-volume proxies → cap candidates to O(k) → use CELF lazy greedy with timestamped cached marginal gains → reuse arrays/incremental HV where possible → finish with tiny bounded swap search. Vectorize nondomination and avoid per-call object construction to save orders of magnitude in runtime.

Prefer Pareto prefilter + proxy-ranked, CELF lazy greedy with marginal caching, strict caps on shortlist/HV calls, vectorized dominance/distance ops, and tiny first‑improvement swap budgets. Cache current HV and marginal gains, use log/eps for proxy stability, and adapt shortlist size to k. These yield high HV with far fewer expensive hypervolume evaluations.
comprehensive_reflection:
- Keywords: Pareto-filtering, cheap box-volume/diversity proxies, CELF lazy greedy, timestamped cached marginals, adaptive shortlist (O(k) or small const), bounded swap refinement, incremental HV, vectorized dominance
- Advice: Pre-filter dominated points, rank by fast proxy (vectorized/MC), shortlist adaptively, compute exact marginal HV only on shortlist with cached/incremental updates (CELF), finish with a tiny budgeted swap local search.
- Avoid: Per-candidate full HV scans, rebuilding objects/arrays in loops, unbounded shortlist/HV calls, nondeterministic seeding, and unbatched computations.
- Explanation: Reduces expensive hypervolume evaluations and memory churn while keeping high selection quality via focused exact HV work, batched/vectorized ops, and cheap refinements.
